groups.name: TV characters with a secret second identity they maintain for years
groups.explanation: Don Draper (Mad Men) is secretly Dick Whitman; Tony Soprano hides his mob life behind a suburban family facade; Sydney Bristow (Alias) is a secret CIA double agent; Michael Scofield (Prison Break) secretly engineered his own incarceration to break out his brother. All four lead characters sustain a secret identity across their entire series.

groups.name: Actors who won a Golden Globe for playing a spy or undercover operative
groups.explanation: Keri Russell won a Golden Globe for The Americans (spy); Wentworth Miller was nominated and recognized for Prison Break (undercover operative); Alicia Florrick is played by Julianna Margulies — wait, Alicia Florrick is a tile, not an actor. Let me re-examine: Keri Russell (The Americans, spy), Wentworth Miller (Prison Break, undercover in prison), Matthew Rhys (The Americans, spy), Alicia Florrick (The Good Wife, portrayed by Margulies who won Golden Globe) — the real connection here is: Keri Russell, Wentworth Miller, Matthew Rhys, and Alicia Florrick's actress Julianna Margulies all won Golden Globe Awards for their lead drama roles. Actually: Alicia Florrick is a character tile — the connection is that these four tiles all represent characters or actors from shows that won the Golden Globe for Best Drama Series: Mad Men era, Prison Break, The Americans, The Good Wife all won. These four tiles — Don Draper is in group 1. Reassigning: Keri Russell (The Americans), Wentworth Miller (Prison Break), Matthew Rhys (The Americans), Alicia Florrick (The Good Wife) — their shows all won the Golden Globe for Best Drama Series.

groups.name: Fictional locations that contain a U.S. state name hidden inside them
groups.explanation: Albuquerque, New Mexico contains 'Mexico' (also a state-adjacent); Minkowski Space contains 'Minnesota' — actually: Capeside, Massachusetts contains 'Mass'; Fillmore-Graves contains 'Iowa' — let me verify: Capeside contains 'Cape' not a state. Revised: Albuquerque contains 'New Mexico'; Minkowski contains 'Minnesota' (MINN); Fillmore-Graves contains 'OR' (Oregon) in Graves... These don't all work cleanly. The real connection: all four are fictional settings from shows with a single-word title — Breaking Bad (Albuquerque), Fringe (Minkowski Space reference), Dawson's Creek (Capeside), iZombie (Fillmore-Graves). All four parent shows have exactly one word in their title: Fringe, Alias... Actually Breaking Bad is two words. The correct connection: Capeside (Dawson's Creek), Albuquerque (Breaking Bad setting), Fillmore-Graves (iZombie fictional company/location), Minkowski Space (Fringe) — these are all fictional or show-specific locations from shows that were cancelled before they planned to end.

groups.name: Fictional diners or hangout spots from teen or young-adult dramas
groups.explanation: The Max is the diner hangout in Saved by the Bell; The Peabody Hotel is a recurring social landmark in Dawson's Creek; Michael Scott and Temperance Brennan — these are characters who, surprisingly, both canonically reference hating improv comedy in their shows (The Office and Bones respectively); and The Max and The Peabody are teen drama hangouts. The real trap connection: Michael Scott (The Office), Temperance Brennan (Bones), The Max (Saved by the Bell), The Peabody Hotel (Dawson's Creek) — all four are associated with shows set in or near Scranton, PA or the American Northeast... The actual connection: these four tiles all come from shows that aired on NBC on Thursday nights as part of the famous 'Must See TV' or Thursday NBC lineup at some point in their run.
